content:
Every team I’ve watched build a Text-to-SQL system makes the same move. They read the leaderboard papers, spot schema linking and self-consistency and multi-step decomposition, and bolt all of it on at once. Then they stare at a tripled token bill and an accuracy number that barely budged, and they wonder where the magic went.
I’ve spent enough time wiring natural-language-to-SQL onto messy production databases to have a strong prior here. Most of the popular recipe is theater. It has the look of rigor and the price tag of rigor. The payoff is where it falls short.
A new benchmark just put hard numbers behind that hunch, and the numbers are sharper than I expected.
The paper is How Far Do On-Prem Open LLMs Get on Text-to-SQL? by Vladimir Beskorovainyi. It tests the cheap accuracy tricks honestly, on open-weight models you can actually run on your own GPUs, and it reports the failures in full. No cloud API, no fine-tuning, no leaderboard-chasing. It asks one clean question:
Which of these tricks is worth paying for when the model has to live…
